narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown impressive strengths like your undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical student's 3.21, and a family income in the high bracket that reflects stability many applicants don't have. You're planning to attend full-time, just like over 90% of students in the dataset, and your LSAT score of 32 is respectable, though below the average of 36.70. However, your Year 1 school decile of 2 and Year 3 decile of 2 are both notably lower than the averages of around 5.5-5.7, placing you in the lower performance tiers compared to peers. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated your probability of failing at 45.5%—a risk level that tipped the scales toward rejecting your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as it strongly signaled challenges in sustaining strong academic performance relative to others. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive pull, suggesting it helps toward passing by showing your college-level capability exceeds the norm. Your LSAT score and high family income also nudged the prediction toward success, with the LSAT reflecting solid reasoning skills and family resources providing an advantage over typical applicants. Smaller influences included your lower Year 1 decile, which slightly raised concerns, while full-time status offered a minor boost aligned with most successful students. Overall, while your GPA and background shine brighter than average, the model weighed your weaker school deciles—especially in Year 3—as the dominant risk for bar exam struggles compared to typical admits. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to or above average, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or explanations in a new application, and highlighting any unique experiences as a Black woman that demonstrate your resilience and potential. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—keep pursuing your dream with that determination. We're rooting for you.
